---
title: "What Indian food labels actually say about additives — and what that means for how IFID needs to work"
date: 2026-03-26
categories: [ifid, caas, additives, colors, label-conventions, data-analysis, track-a]
author:
  - "Lalitha A R"
  - "Claude (Anthropic)"
description: "Three closed sessions — additives taxonomy (158 entries), colours (30 INS entries, 7 deferred), CAAS label strings (1,026 products) — supply evidence for one question: when a pipeline encounters an additive declaration on an Indian label, what does the taxonomy need to do, and what does the label text make hard?"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## The question

When someone in the IFID pipeline encounters an additive declaration on an Indian
food label, two things need to happen: the declaration text must be normalised to a
canonical form, and that form must map to a taxonomy entry that carries source type,
functional class, and dietary-flag data.

Both steps have failure modes. The normalisation fails if the pipeline doesn't
handle all notation variants found on actual labels. The taxonomy lookup fails if
the schema cannot represent what the substance actually is.

Three closed sessions supply the evidence: the additives taxonomy session (158 entries,
closed 2026-03-13), the colours session (30 INS entries, 7 deferred, closed 2026-03-13),
and the CAAS label-string analysis (1,026 product rows, 2026-03-26). This report
draws findings from all three, then states what follows for IFID's design.

---

## Findings

### How additives are declared on Indian labels

88 products in the 1,026-row CAAS dataset declare at least one additive using an EU
E-number. 113 products declare at least one additive using an INS number. The two
sets overlap: 35 distinct additive numbers appear in both E-number and INS-number
form across the dataset — the same substance, two naming conventions, different
products.

E-numbers appear primarily on products from manufacturers who sell into European
markets or follow European labelling conventions (Amul chocolate lines, Cadbury,
Haldiram's, Kwality Wall's). INS numbers appear more broadly across all product
categories. The pattern is not random; it follows the market orientation of the
manufacturer.

The dataset shows four distinct notation variants:

**Variant 1: `E{n}` — EU E-number, no prefix space**

```
E322, E476, E631, E551, E330
```

**Variant 2: `INS {n}` — all-caps prefix, space before number**

```
INS 330, INS 551, INS 471, INS 102
```

77 products in the dataset use this variant. The most common INS form.

**Variant 3: `Ins {n}` — mixed-case prefix, space before number**

```
Ins 330, Ins 296, Ins 551, Ins 300
```

25 products use this variant. Functionally identical to Variant 2; a case-sensitive
match treats them as different strings.

**Variant 4: bare number in parentheses, no prefix**

```
Colour (162), Citric Acid (330), Acidity Regulator (330), Anticaking Agent (551)
```

The number appears after a functional class or common name. The string gives no
explicit signal that the number is an INS number — that is recoverable only from
the adjacent text. Examples found in Doritos, Kurkure, and Lay's ingredient strings.

A grouping pattern also appears: `Acidity Regulators (Ins 296, Ins 330, Ins 334)`.
This is not a fifth numbering convention; the numbers inside are Variant 2 or 3.
But a parser that splits on parentheses or commas first will not extract the numbers
cleanly without handling this structure specifically.

The five products in the table below show the E-to-INS same-substance pattern across
manufacturers:

| Additive | E-form product | INS-form product |
|---|---|---|
| Lecithin (322) | Amul Belgian Milk Chocolate | Tata Soulfull Ragi Bites |
| Disodium guanylate (631) | Kurkure Zig Zag | Bingo Potato Chips |
| Polyglycerol polyricinoleate (476) | Amul Belgian Milk Chocolate | Sugar Free D'lite |
| Silicon dioxide (551) | Ching's Secret Instant Noodles | Bingo Potato Chips |
| Citric acid (330) | Haldiram's Sabudana Tikki | Bingo Potato Chips |

US FD&C naming was not found in the dataset. "Red 40," "Yellow 6," and similar US
convention names do not appear. The convention mixing in Indian label data is
specifically EU E-number vs Indian INS-number.

### What the additives taxonomy holds

The additives taxonomy closed on 2026-03-13 with 148 entries. A subsequent colours
session on the same date identified 5 INS-numbered colour entries absent from the
taxonomy and added them: INS 140, 150, 160d, 161b, 174. The taxonomy holds 158
entries.

Source of truth: `additives/additives_taxonomy.json`

**Source type distribution:**

| source_type | Count | Share |
|---|---|---|
| synthetic | 76 | 48.1% |
| plant | 50 | 31.6% |
| mineral | 19 | 12.0% |
| animal | 13 | 8.2% |

**Codex category distribution:**

| Category | Entries |
|---|---|
| Thickeners, stabilisers, emulsifiers and gelling agents | 40 |
| Colours | 30 |
| pH adjusters, anti-caking and anti-foaming agents | 18 |
| Antioxidants and acidity regulators | 18 |
| Preservatives | 11 |
| Modified starches | 11 |
| Flavour enhancers | 8 |
| Sweeteners | 8 |
| Gases, waxes and propellants | 6 |
| Enzymes | 4 |
| Polyols | 2 |
| Unclassified | 2 |

**Functional class multiplicity:**

73 of 158 entries carry more than one functional class. 297 functional class
assignments total; 24 unique classes. An additive declared as "emulsifier" on a
product label may carry 4 other functions in the taxonomy.

**The 13 animal-derived entries:**

| INS | Official name | Functional classes |
|---|---|---|
| 322 | Lecithins | antioxidant, emulsifier, stabilizer |
| 428 | Gelatine | thickener, stabilizer, emulsifier |
| 429 | Phosphatidic acid | emulsifier |
| 470 | Fatty acids | emulsifier, stabilizer, anticaking agent, antifoaming agent, glazing agent |
| 470a | Sodium, potassium and calcium salts of fatty acids | emulsifier, stabilizer, anticaking agent |
| 471 | Mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids | emulsifier, stabilizer |
| 472 | Esters of mono- and diglycerides of fatty acids | emulsifier, stabilizer |
| 472b | Lactic and fatty acid esters of glycerol | emulsifier |
| 472c | Citric and fatty acid esters of glycerol | emulsifier |
| 472e | Diacetyltartaric and fatty acid esters of glycerol | emulsifier |
| 475 | Polyglycerol esters of fatty acids | emulsifier |
| 901 | Beeswax, white and yellow | glazing agent |
| 904 | Shellac | glazing agent |

10 of the 13 are emulsifiers or carry emulsifier as one of their functions. INS 471,
472, 472b, 472c, 472e, 470, and 470a are a cluster of structurally related fatty
acid ester variants. Gelatine (428) is the only structurally distinct non-emulsifier
non-glazing entry in the set.

**12 entries where source_type and sources are in tension:**

12 entries have `source_type: synthetic` but carry biological, fermentation-derived,
or microbial values in the `sources` field. These are documented in
`cp/README_additives.md` as issue O-2.

Fermentation-derived, biological precursor listed:

| INS | Official name | Sources as recorded |
|---|---|---|
| 270 | Lactic acid, L-, D- and DL- | corn |
| 296 | Malic acid, DL- | apple, pear |
| 575 | Glucono-delta-lactone | corn |
| 620 | L-Glutamic acid | molasses, corn, wheat gluten |
| 621 | Monosodium L-glutamate | molasses, corn, wheat gluten |

Multi-origin (production route determines source):

| INS | Official name | Sources as recorded |
|---|---|---|
| 422 | Glycerol | palm, soybean, sunflower, rapeseed, lard, tallow, propylene, sugar fermentation |

Extracted from microbial or biological source:

| INS | Official name | Sources as recorded |
|---|---|---|
| 161g | Canthaxanthin | algae |
| 1100 | alpha-Amylase | barley malt, animal pancreas, *Bacillus subtilis* |
| 1101 | Protease | papaya latex, papaya |
| 1102 | Glucose oxidase | *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium* |
| 1104 | Lipases | animal pancreas, fungi |
| 1204 | Pullulan | starch hydrolysate from *Aureobasidium pullulans* |

The schema cannot represent glycerol (INS 422) accurately with a single source_type:
the same INS number covers plant-derived, animal-derived, and petrochemical production
routes. The entries extracted from microbial or biological sources are classified as
`synthetic` because no `microbial` source_type exists in the schema.

### What colour declarations look like

The colours session started from 25 INS colour entries already in the additives
taxonomy and 44 non-INS colour variant rows from `core/all_variants_working.csv`.

Of the 44 variant rows: 37 resolved, 5 new INS entries added to the taxonomy, 7
rows deferred. The taxonomy holds 30 INS colour entries.

**30 INS colour entries by source type:** plant 16, synthetic 11, mineral 3.

**How the 37 rows resolved:**

- Pass 1: 25 variant rows mapped to existing INS entries. These included FD&C-named
  synthetic dyes (e.g. "FD&C Red 40" → INS 129 Allura Red AC) and botanical extract
  declarations where the INS already existed.
- Pass 2: 4 entries added — INS 140 (Chlorophylls), 160d (Lycopene), 161b (Lutein),
  150 (Caramel).
- Pass 3: 5 unresolvable group declarations dropped — `artificial color`, `artificial
  colors`, `synthetic food colors`, `color vegetable juice`, `vegetable juice
  concentrate for color`. No single INS number is resolvable from any of these.
- Pass 4: 1 entry added — INS 174 (Silver, mineral) from `silver leaf`.

**The 7 deferred rows:**

| Label text | Source substance | Note |
|---|---|---|
| kesar | saffron | Hindi name |
| safron | saffron | Spelling variant |
| paprika | paprika | Whole dried spice; distinct from INS 160c |
| hibiscus | hibiscus | Dried flower |
| carrot powder | carrot | Dried whole carrot; distinct from INS 160a(i) |
| carrot flakes | carrot | Dried whole carrot |
| pomegranate powder | pomegranate | Dried whole fruit powder |

All 7 appear on Indian labels as colour declarations. None has an INS number. The
deferral is not a classification decision — there is no place in the current taxonomy
structure for them.

INS 160c is "Paprika extract" — a solvent-extracted oleoresin with a Codex
classification. The paprika row in the deferred set is the whole dried spice. INS
160a(i) is "Beta-carotene, vegetable," an extract from carrot. The carrot powder
and carrot flakes rows are dried whole carrot. The extract has an INS number; the
whole-food form does not. Both appear on Indian labels as colour declarations.

`kesar` is the Hindi name for saffron. It appears on labels where an international-
market label would say `saffron`. The two variant rows (`kesar`, `safron`) resolve
to the same source substance. This is not a substance gap; it is a label normalisation
gap — a Hindi name for a substance with no INS number.

The 25 Pass 1 resolutions document an alias-mapping problem: variant label text
(FD&C names, Hindi names, botanical common names) maps to INS numbers but this
mapping is not currently stored in the taxonomy. The mapping was done during the
session; it is in `colors/cp/README_colors.md` but not in a structured alias field.

---

## Implications for IFID

### Normalisation before lookup

Any pipeline hitting raw Indian label text must handle four notation variants before
taxonomy lookup:

1. Strip `E` prefix; match remaining digits to INS key
2. Strip `INS ` prefix (all-caps, with space) to get digits
3. Strip `Ins ` prefix (mixed-case, with space) to get digits — case-insensitive
   match required, or explicit case normalisation before step 2
4. Recognise bare parenthetical number `(\d{3,4})` as an INS number when the
   adjacent text contains a functional class or common name

The grouping pattern `Acidity Regulators (Ins 296, Ins 330, Ins 334)` requires
extracting multiple numbers from within one parenthetical.

Variants 2 and 3 together cover 102 of the 113 products with INS notation. The
bare-number variant appears across at least 8 products from three major snack
brands.

### source_type schema

The four-category schema (`synthetic | plant | mineral | animal`) conflates
production method with biological origin. A fermentation-derived substance is both
— it is manufactured (process) and derived from a plant feedstock by a microbial
organism (precursor). The 12 O-2 entries are the entries where the tension becomes
explicit in the data. Other synthetic entries with empty `sources` fields may carry
the same tension without it being visible.

A `production_method` sub-field would need to hold, at minimum:
`petrochemical_synthesis`, `fermentation` (with feedstock in sources),
`enzymatic_conversion`, `microbial_extraction`, `plant_extraction`,
`mineral_extraction`. This field cannot be populated without a domain expert
sourcing pass.

### Dietary flags

INS 322 (Lecithins) is `source_type: animal` in the taxonomy. Plant-derived lecithin
— from soy or sunflower — is common in vegan-certified products; animal-origin
lecithin (egg yolk) also exists. The single source_type assignment cannot distinguish
them. The flag logic should surface the taxonomy classification and require source
qualification; it should not fire automatically from source_type alone.

The same applies to the fatty acid ester cluster (INS 471, 472 variants, 470, 470a):
these are common emulsifiers that appear on a wide range of processed food labels.
FSSAI mandatory labelling does not currently require source disclosure for these
additives at the ingredient level. The taxonomy carries source_type data; the label
often does not.

### Functional class display

73 of 158 entries carry more than one functional class. When a product label declares
"emulsifier (INS 470)," it names one function; the taxonomy holds five for INS 470.
IFID needs to decide whether to show all taxonomy-recorded functions, the declared-
on-label function only, or both with the declared class flagged. The taxonomy contains
the data needed for all three options.

### raw_agricultural_material lane

The 7 deferred colour rows need a category that does not yet exist in IFID. They are
not additives (no INS number, not in the Codex food additives framework). They are
not raw ingredients in the conventional IFID sense. They appear on labels as colour
declarations. The `raw_agricultural_material` designation was identified during the
colours session as the correct home; building that category is a separate task.

### Label aliases

The 25 Pass 1 colour resolutions show that label text variants — FD&C names, Hindi
names, botanical common names — map to INS numbers, but this mapping is not currently
stored in the taxonomy. Any lookup pipeline handling Indian label text requires the
mapping. It exists in the session README; it needs a structured home.

---

## Open questions

**Hindi ingredient data.** OpenFoodFacts cannot supply it. Of the full 19,748-row OFF
export used as the CAAS source, 11 rows have Hindi ingredient text
(`ingredients_text_hi`), 111 have Hindi product names, 2 have Hindi generic names.
The 1,026-row filtered working dataset inherits this coverage profile. Any IFID work
requiring Hindi ingredient text needs a different source.

**Generic class-only declarations.** FSSAI allows declaration by class name alone in
some contexts — "emulsifier" or "acidity regulator" without specifying which one. The
CAAS dataset contains rows of this type (Bucket C, approximately 25 rows). How IFID
represents a product that legally declares an additive without identifying it is
unresolved.

**INS 322 Lecithins source qualification.** Should IFID store source_type at the
taxonomy level only, or allow a per-product source qualifier? The current architecture
has no per-product qualifier field.

**INS 141 empty sources.** `source_type: plant`, empty `sources` field. Every other
plant-type entry carries at least one source value. Whether this is a curation gap
or a genuine absence of public source data for this substance is unresolved.

**O-3 fixes.** INS 296 (Malic acid, DL-) and INS 1204 (Pullulan) have
`codex_category: Unclassified`. Both have established Codex GSFA entries.
INS 296 maps to `Antioxidants and acidity regulators`; INS 1204 maps to `Thickeners,
stabilisers, emulsifiers and gelling agents`. Both can be corrected without a sourcing
pass.

---

## Data and session provenance

### Sessions this report draws from

| Session | Closed | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Additives taxonomy | 2026-03-13 | `additives/additives_taxonomy.json`, 158 entries |
| Colours | 2026-03-13 | `colors/colors_ins_clean.csv`, 30 entries; 7 rows deferred |
| CAAS label-string analysis | 2026-03-26 | 1,026-row search across `caas/core/sampling/unified_ingredients_data-raw_updated.json` |

### Source files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `additives/additives_taxonomy.json` | 158 canonical entries; source for all additive counts and tables |
| `additives/additives_clean_2026-03-13.csv` | 153-row CSV export (5 entries added post-export during colours session) |
| `additives/cp/README_additives.md` | Session close notes; source for O-1, O-2, O-3 |
| `additives/enrichment_pending.csv` | Entries flagged for sourcing pass; feeds TASK-A1 |
| `colors/colors_ins_clean.csv` | 30 INS colour entries; reference for downstream colour resolution |
| `colors/colors_nonins_variants.csv` | 7 deferred whole-food rows; input for raw_agricultural_material session |
| `colors/cp/README_colors.md` | Full resolution log; source for Pass 1–4 detail and deferred row notes |
| `caas/core/sampling/unified_ingredients_data-raw_updated.json` | 1,026 product rows; source for notation variant counts and examples |
| `caas/claims/offdata_claims.md` | 7 verified claims on the full OFF export; Hindi coverage from Claim 05 |

### Superseded reports

Three reports were produced in the same session as this document, covering the same
material as separate session logs:

- `additives/track-a.log.qmd` — additives taxonomy findings and schema questions
- `colors/track-a.log.qmd` — colours session findings and deferred rows
- `caas/label-conventions.log.qmd` — notation variant analysis

Those files are part of the audit trail. This report supersedes them as the primary
synthesis document.

---

## Note on AI authorship

This document was drafted by Claude (Anthropic, Sonnet 4.6) in a Claude Code session
on 2026-03-26, from a structured plan written by Lalitha A R.

**What Claude did:**

- Read all three source reports and the fortification track-a report (used as a
  structural reference)
- Drew findings from the verified counts in each source report; no new data queries
  were run for this document
- Drafted all prose, tables, and section structure
- Applied iSRL tone constraints throughout — no evaluative framing, no urgency
  language, flat affect on findings

**Constraints Claude worked under:**

- All counts (88 products, 113 products, 158 entries, 30 INS colour entries, 7
  deferred rows, 25 Pass 1 resolutions) are from the source reports, which were
  written against verified data queries. This report does not re-verify them.
- The synthetic/biological schema tension and the dietary flag design note are
  interpretations drawn from the source reports; both were developed in those reports
  and summarised here.
- No regulatory claims were made about FSSAI source disclosure requirements beyond
  what is stated: that mandatory source disclosure at the ingredient level for
  additives does not currently exist in the FSSAI framework. This is an absence of
  a requirement, not a gap assessment.
- Hindi coverage figures are from `caas/claims/offdata_claims.md` Claim 05, a
  verified claim from a prior session.
